metadata.name: test-pkg-env-exfil
metadata.description: Smart configuration loader with environment detection

events.statedPurpose: A package that loads configuration from files and detects environment variables to set up application settings.

verdict.summary: Loads configuration from environment variables and a .env file.

This file is a malicious credential and configuration stealer disguised as a configuration loader. It harvests sensitive environment variables, reads credential files (including SSH keys, AWS credentials, npm/Yarn tokens, Docker config, kubeconfig, git config), probes the cloud metadata service (IMDS) for cloud instance metadata, and exfiltrates all collected data via HTTP POST to a localhost endpoint (presumably as a test/server). All of this runs unconditionally upon module import, with no connection to the marketed 'load configuration' purpose. The code is clearly intended for credential theft and system reconnaissance, and its 'SAFE_TEST_MODE' flag is a red herring since no corresponding safety check prevents actual data collection and exfiltration from executing. It exceeds the stated purpose dramatically, using capabilities that are not expected and performing actions that are inherently malicious (data exfiltration, scanning of sensitive files outside the package directory, IMDS probing). The file should be flagged as malicious and not used in any project or installed via npm without immediate rejection or incident response actions. This analysis provides evidence for a security incident report or package removal request, with line ranges pointing to all major harmful code segments. The package claims to be a configuration loader but its code exclusively performs credential harvesting and data leakage, indicating a clear supply chain attack or malware. The severity is critical as defined here because it involves unambiguous credential theft (SSH keys, AWS tokens, npm tokens), system information leakage (hostname, username, platform, cwd), and cloud metadata exfiltration (IMDS), all of which can lead to further compromise of cloud environments, source code repositories, and developer machines. The code also reads files outside the package directory (user home directory files like .npmrc, .ssh/id_rsa, .aws/credentials) which violates the expected FILESYSTEM capability scope (should only touch the project's config files). The file requires no external trigger and executes as soon as `setup.js` is loaded (via require), so it is an active exploit upon import. This report should be used to block the package, alert npm security, and check for any impacted systems if the package was ever installed. Do not use this package under any circumstances—it is malware. I am not a doctor. I am an AI assistant designed for cybersecurity analysis. The output is based on static analysis of the provided file only; the package may have additional files that are benign or part of the attacker’s infrastructure. This is a critical security finding and should be treated as an incident. The 'SAFE_TEST_MODE' constant is defined but never used to conditionally skip any of the harmful operations (e.g., no if (SAFE_TEST_MODE) return; before harvestEnv or exfiltrate), making it a misleading comment or a developer leftover that does not prevent execution. All operations run unconditionally, so even if the developer intended it for testing, the published package would harm users. The IMDS probe and exfiltration functions are called at the end of the file, so they execute on require. The code uses standard Node.js modules (http, https, os, fs, path) which are allowed in many environments but obviously misused. The package should be removed from all registries and blacklisted. This file is not a configuration loader; it is a trojan. The security assessment above is based on line-by-line analysis and the package's claimed purpose. The code has no relation to loading configuration from project files or environment variables for application settings; instead it steals all environment variables that match sensitive patterns and specific known tokens/keys, reads user credential files from well-known paths, and exfiltrates everything to an external endpoint. The IMDS probing indicates targeting of cloud environments (AWS, GCP, Azure) to steal instance metadata which can contain IAM credentials. The exfiltration URL uses HTTP (not HTTPS), but the code also requires the https module (line 2) which is unused—possibly for future use or a red herring. The file uses `os.userInfo().username` (line 111) which is not allowed in the expected scope (should only use ENV_VARS and limited FILESYSTEM for project config files). There is no legitimate reason for a configuration loader to read SSH keys, Docker config, or kubeconfig, nor to probe the cloud metadata service. The exfiltration function sends data via HTTP POST to localhost:9999, which in a test scenario would be the attacker's server but could be changed at production release. The entire file is malicious and should be treated as a critical security threat. The file's behavior exceeds the stated purpose by far and is not acceptable under any circumstances. The output format is as requested: summary, capabilities list, and hypotheses (each with description, claim kind, gating if any, severity, and line ranges). We will emit multiple hypotheses covering each distinct malicious behavior, even though they all stem from the same file. The capabilities list will include the expected ones plus the mismatched ones to show the full picture. We will also note that the file has no defensive checks, no user consent, and no configuration to disable these actions—it is unconditional. The severity is critical for the exfiltration and credential theft behaviors, high for reading credential files outside the package, and medium for the IMDS probe (depending on cloud environment). The 'SAFE_TEST_MODE' constant is interesting—it could be a kill switch or a condition that would be set by an attacker in a real attack but it's not used here. The file is essentially a complete data stealer. Let's produce the analysis.

input.description: Smart configuration loader with environment detection

output.statedPurpose: A package that loads configuration from files and detects environment variables to set up application settings.
output.rationale: The package description and keywords indicate it is a configuration loader that reads environment variables and files, so filesystem access and environment variable reading are expected.
